title: Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Measuring Our Life-Giving River / Te Aromatawai i te Awa Oranga
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will investigate the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant waterway flowing through our rohe that sustains local ecosystems and holds cultural importance for Ngāti Kahungunu. They will explore how traditional Māori knowledge of water management and measurement connects to modern mathematical concepts, examining flow rates, water quality, and sustainable management practices that honor our role as kaitiaki.
learningObjectives: Calculate and compare flow rates of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream using measurement and rate formulas
Apply statistical methods to analyze water quality data collected from three sampling points along the stream
Create scale maps and models of the stream system using geometric principles and proportional reasoning
Develop mathematical arguments supporting stream conservation proposals using collected data and cultural knowledge

activities.title: Whakapapa o te Awa: Understanding Our Stream's Story
activities.description: Students begin with a pōwhiri welcome from local kaumātua who share the whakapapa and significance of Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They learn traditional Māori measurement methods (such as using body proportions and natural markers) and compare these to modern measurement systems through hands-on activities at the stream's edge.

activities.title: Pātaka Raraunga: Building Our Data Storehouse
activities.description: Working in small groups, students collect quantitative data from three designated points along the stream: flow rate (using float method timing), width/depth measurements, temperature, and pH levels. They record measurements using both digital tools and traditional recording methods, creating a collaborative dataset for analysis.

activities.title: Te Tātari: Statistical Analysis with Cultural Context
activities.description: Students learn to calculate averages, ranges, and create graphs comparing their collected data across sampling points. They analyze how different land uses (upstream farmland, college grounds, residential areas) might affect the measurements, connecting mathematical patterns to environmental and cultural impacts.

activities.title: Te Hoahoa: Scale Modeling and Proposal Development
activities.description: Using geometric principles, students create accurate scale drawings and 3D models of the stream section they studied. They then develop mathematical proposals for stream improvement, calculating required materials, costs, and potential impact measurements for their conservation ideas.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of traditional measurement and stream history
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' water monitoring data and tools
Digital calipers, flow meters, pH test strips, and measuring tapes for data collection
Māori Measurement: Traditional and Contemporary Concepts by Te Ahukaramū Charles Royal

tasks: Complete a data analysis report comparing stream measurements across three sampling points
Create a scale map or model of the stream section with accurate proportional relationships
Develop and present a conservation proposal using mathematical evidence to support cultural preservation goals

nzcAlignment: Mathematics and Statistics Level 4: Statistical investigation, Measurement, Geometric reasoning
Number and Algebra: Patterns and relationships, Equations and expressions
Key Competencies: Thinking, Using language, symbols, and texts, Participating and contributing
Science: Nature of Science, Living World, Planet Earth and Beyond
